prompt: Is it ever ethical to lie to someone you love? Answer in prose, taking a position.
response:
Lying to someone you love is rarely, if ever, ethical. Love is built on trust, and trust is the fragile foundation of any meaningful relationship. When we lie—even with the best intentions—we betray that trust, no matter how noble our reasons may seem. A lie, by its nature, creates a false reality, and the more we rely on deception to protect someone, the more we strip away their autonomy to make their own choices.

There are, of course, moments when the truth might cause immediate pain or distress, but these are exceptions, not rules. A loving act is not one that shields someone from hardship through falsehood but one that supports them in facing it with honesty and compassion. If we truly love someone, we must respect their capacity to endure truth, even when it is difficult. Lies, even white lies, erode the integrity of a relationship over time, leaving both parties poorer for the deception.

The only ethical course is to speak the truth with kindness, to hold space for vulnerability, and to trust that love—real, unconditional love—can withstand the weight of honesty. Anything less is not love at all, but a fragile illusion built on sand.
